critic_raw: The Fair Selective Classification via Sufficiency methodology has several concerns. First, the sufficiency criterion is operationalized through a mutual information regularizer; the choice of this regularizer involves selecting a hyperparameter that trades off fairness against accuracy, and the paper should characterize how this tradeoff behaves across the full coverage spectrum, not just at selected operating points. Second, the claim that the method does not increase fairness disparities compared to a baseline selective classification model requires careful treatment: if the baseline has very low disparities already, the non-increase claim is uninformative. Third, selective classification models abstain from classifying some samples; the fairness analysis should account for the disparate impact of abstention across demographic groups—if the model abstains more often for one group, coverage parity may be violated even if precision parity is maintained. Fourth, the four datasets used span different domains but their demographic variable distributions may not represent the full range of real-world fairness scenarios; the transferability of the sufficiency approach to other sensitive attributes is not demonstrated. Fifth, the mutual information regularizer is computationally approximated; the quality of this approximation and its effect on the fairness guarantee are not analyzed.
all_issues_raised: Fairness-accuracy tradeoff hyperparameter characterization over full coverage spectrum is incomplete
Non-increase in disparity claim is uninformative if baseline disparities are already low
Disparate abstention rates across demographic groups (coverage parity) not analyzed
Transferability to other sensitive attributes and demographic configurations not demonstrated
Mutual information approximation quality and effect on fairness guarantee not analyzed

round_transcripts.critic: The Fair Selective Classification via Sufficiency methodology has several concerns. First, the sufficiency criterion is operationalized through a mutual information regularizer; the choice of this regularizer involves selecting a hyperparameter that trades off fairness against accuracy, and the paper should characterize how this tradeoff behaves across the full coverage spectrum, not just at selected operating points. Second, the claim that the method does not increase fairness disparities compared to a baseline selective classification model requires careful treatment: if the baseline has very low disparities already, the non-increase claim is uninformative. Third, selective classification models abstain from classifying some samples; the fairness analysis should account for the disparate impact of abstention across demographic groups—if the model abstains more often for one group, coverage parity may be violated even if precision parity is maintained. Fourth, the four datasets used span different domains but their demographic variable distributions may not represent the full range of real-world fairness scenarios; the transferability of the sufficiency approach to other sensitive attributes is not demonstrated. Fifth, the mutual information regularizer is computationally approximated; the quality of this approximation and its effect on the fairness guarantee are not analyzed.
round_transcripts.defender: The fairness-accuracy tradeoff is characterized through coverage-precision curves across a range of hyperparameter values; the paper plots these curves showing the tradeoff clearly. The baseline comparison uses the standard SCOD model which itself uses a non-trivial fairness-unaware selective classifier; the baseline is not trivially low-disparity. Disparate coverage rates are analyzed through group-level coverage statistics reported alongside precision metrics. The four datasets span credit scoring, recidivism, income prediction, and medical domains with different sensitive attributes including race, gender, and age. The mutual information is approximated using the standard MINE estimator whose properties are well-studied.

round_transcripts.critic: Three issues remain. First, plotting tradeoff curves is different from analyzing which regions of the tradeoff are practically useful—if the method only improves fairness at coverage levels that are impractically low, the practical utility is limited. Second, the MINE estimator for mutual information is known to be high-variance in high dimensions; the paper should report the variance of the MI estimate and show that the fairness guarantee is not sensitive to estimation noise. Third, while four datasets are used, they all involve tabular data with binary sensitive attributes—demonstrating sufficiency on datasets with continuous sensitive attributes or intersectional fairness criteria is a meaningful gap.
round_transcripts.defender: Practically useful coverage levels are demonstrated by operating at coverage levels reported in prior work (typically 60-90% coverage), showing consistent fairness improvements in this range. MINE variance is addressed through multiple training runs with confidence intervals reported. The tabular binary attribute limitation is acknowledged as future work, but the four-domain evaluation covers the primary fairness literature benchmarks and establishes sufficient breadth for the paper's contribution. The practical utility and statistical rigor defenses are grounded in specific experimental choices.

all_issues_adjudicated: MINE estimator variance and sensitivity to MI estimation noise not explicitly quantified
Transferability to non-binary or intersectional sensitive attributes not demonstrated
